_meta.title: Rust for High Performance — UnsafeCell, MaybeUninit, alignment

questions.question: What is the key difference between UnsafeCell<T> and RefCell<T> at runtime?
options: UnsafeCell adds a borrow counter; RefCell does not
RefCell adds runtime borrow-checking overhead; UnsafeCell has zero runtime overhead
UnsafeCell is thread-safe; RefCell is not
They are identical — UnsafeCell is just the unsafe version of RefCell
questions.explanation: RefCell maintains a borrow state counter and performs runtime checks on every borrow()/borrow_mut() call, causing a branch + atomic overhead. UnsafeCell has zero runtime overhead — it's just a raw pointer dereference — but requires you to manually guarantee no aliasing violations.

questions.question: Why does MaybeUninit<T> exist instead of just using mem::uninitialized()?
options: MaybeUninit is faster because it uses SIMD instructions
mem::uninitialized() was removed because reading uninitialized memory is always instant UB; MaybeUninit prevents accidental reads by requiring .write() before .assume_init()
MaybeUninit allows the compiler to optimize the zero-initialization away
They do the same thing; MaybeUninit is just a renamed version
questions.explanation: mem::uninitialized() allowed creating a T value that might not be valid, and reading it was instant UB. The compiler could also miscompile code based on the assumption that the value was valid. MaybeUninit<T> is a wrapper that makes it a type-level error to read the inner value without first calling .write() to initialize it, then .assume_init() with an explicit safety proof.

questions.question: What happens when you take a reference (&T) to a field in a #[repr(C, packed)] struct?
options: It works fine — packed structs just remove padding
The reference may be misaligned, which is undefined behavior in Rust
The compiler automatically copies the field to an aligned temporary
It causes a compilation error in all cases
questions.explanation: Packed structs can place fields at misaligned addresses. In Rust, creating a reference (&T) to a misaligned value is UB because references must always be properly aligned. The correct approach is to use ptr::read_unaligned and ptr::write_unaligned to access packed fields without creating references.

questions.question: When is using unsafe (e.g., get_unchecked) actually worth the risk in performance-critical code?
options: Always — unsafe is always faster than safe Rust
Never — safe Rust can always match unsafe performance with the right abstractions
When profiling shows the safe version is a bottleneck, the unsafe version is measurably faster, and you can wrap it in a small auditable safe API with SAFETY comments
Only when the optimizer fails to remove bounds checks, which is rare
questions.explanation: The correct approach is: (1) Profile to confirm the safe version is actually a bottleneck. (2) Benchmark the unsafe version with black_box to confirm it's measurably faster. (3) Wrap the unsafe in a minimal, well-documented safe API with SAFETY comments. Using unsafe without measurement just adds risk without benefit.

questions.question:
What does Pin

guarantee and why does it matter for self-referential structs?
options: Pin prevents any mutation of the pinned value, making it immutable
Pin guarantees the pointed-to value will not be moved in memory, which is critical when a struct holds pointers into its own fields
Pin is a reference-counting smart pointer like Rc but with move protection
Pin automatically frees memory when the value goes out of scope, preventing leaks
questions.explanation:
Pin

wraps a pointer and guarantees that the pointed-to value will never be moved (its memory address stays stable). This is essential for self-referential structs — if a struct contains a pointer into its own data and the struct is moved, that pointer becomes dangling. Pin prevents this by making moves impossible through the type system.

questions.question: What is std::hint::black_box used for and what happens without it in benchmarks?
options: It adds a delay to simulate real-world latency in microbenchmarks
It tells the optimizer the value may be used in any way, preventing constant-folding and dead-code elimination that would make benchmarks measure nothing
It forces the compiler to generate black-box assembly output for debugging
It marks a function as a performance boundary for the profiler
questions.explanation: black_box is an identity function at runtime but tells the optimizer to assume the value could be used in any way. Without it, the compiler can constant-fold inputs (turning add(5, 7) into 12) or eliminate dead code (removing a computation whose result is never used), making your benchmark measure zero work. black_box prevents these optimizations in microbenchmarks.
